1: ˀal-ˀodot (עַל-אֹדוֹת, concerning/because of) + ˀisha Kushit (אִשָּׁה כֻשִׁית, the Cushite woman). The complaint is ostensibly about Moses' foreign wife but functions as a pretext for challenging his authority (v.2). Kush usually refers to Ethiopia/Nubia; this may be Zipporah (Ex 2:21, from Midian) or a second wife. The specific identity matters less than the target: Moses' unique mediatorial position.
2: raq-bˀmoshe dibber YHWH? (הֲרַק אַךְ-בְּמֹשֶׁה דִּבֶּר יְהוָה) — 'Has the LORD spoken only through Moses?' The challenge to prophetic monopoly is a claim for equal standing. But the end of the verse is chilling: wayishmaˁ YHWH (וַיִּשְׁמַע יְהוָה, 'and YHWH heard it'). The challenge that seems to widen prophetic access inadvertently invites divine adjudication of the claim.
3: wehˀish Mosheh ˁanav meˀod (וְהָאִישׁ מֹשֶׁה עָנָו מְאֹד) — 'And the man Moses was very humble/meek, more than any person on the face of the earth.' ˁAnav (עָנָו) = meek, humble, poor in spirit. This is the narrator's parenthetical defense of Moses: his non-response to the attack was not weakness but the most profound meekness. Jesus cites this attribute for himself: 'I am meek (praus) and lowly in heart' (Matt 11:29); he blesses 'the meek' (praus) in Matt 5:5. The description of Moses is the background text for Jesus' self-description.
4: YHWH calls all three — Moses, Aaron, Miriam — to the tent of meeting: tsˀu shloshtekem (צְאוּ שְׁלָשְׁתְּכֶם, go out, you three). The summons is sovereign; the disputants do not come on their own initiative. When divine authority is at stake, YHWH settles the matter directly rather than leaving it to human deliberation.
5: YHWH descends in a pillar of cloud and stands at the tent entrance: ˁamud heˁanan (עַמּוּד הֶעָנָן, pillar of cloud). The theophanic presence is the visual guarantee of what follows. The cloud column that guided Israel through the wilderness now serves as the venue for the judgment of prophetic claims. The same presence that guides also adjudicates.
6: im-yihyeh nˀviˀakhem YHWH — if there is a prophet among you, YHWH makes himself known in marˀah (מַרְאָה, vision) and speaks in ḥalom (חֲלוֹם, dream). These are the two standard modes of prophetic revelation for ordinary prophets: visual and nocturnal. The distinction in vv.6-8 establishes a three-tiered prophetic epistemology: ordinary prophets (dream/vision), Moses (face to face, form seen), and YHWH himself.
7: loˀ-ken ˁavdi Mosheh / bˀkhol-beiti neˀeman huˀ (לֹא-כֵן עַבְדִּי מֹשֶׁה / בְּכָל-בֵּיתִי נֶאֱמָן הוּא) — 'Not so my servant Moses; he is faithful in all my house.' Neˀeman = faithful, trustworthy (niphˁal of ˀaman). The Epistle to the Hebrews cites this directly: 'Moses was faithful (pistos) in all God's house' (Heb 3:2,5) in order to contrast Moses' faithfulness as a servant with Christ's faithfulness as the Son over the house. The verse is the biblical foundation for the Moses/Christ typology in Hebrews 3.
8: peh ˀel-peh adabber-bo (פֶּה אֶל-פֶּה אֲדַבֶּר-בּוֹ) — 'mouth to mouth I speak with him.' Direct, unmediated speech — not in visions or dreams but face to face. utemunat YHWH yabit (וּתְמֻנַת יְהוָה יַבִּיט) — 'he sees the form/figure of YHWH.' Temunah = shape, form, figure. The same word appears in Deut 4:12,15 where Israel saw no form at Sinai. Moses saw what Israel was forbidden to make an image of. His unique access to the divine form is both his privilege and his responsibility.
9: wayˀiḥar-ˀap YHWH (וַיֶּחֱרִי-אַף יְהוָה) — the anger of YHWH blazed and he departed (wayyelek). The divine departure is itself a judgment. When YHWH leaves, the protection of his presence leaves with him. The cloud rising from Miriam is not just withdrawal of blessing — it is the unmasking of disease that the cloud's presence had held in check.
10: mesoraˁat kashsaleg (מְצֹרַעַת כַּשָּׁלֶג, leprous as snow). The tsaraˁat (skin disease) strikes Miriam white as snow — the color associated with bleached death rather than purity here. The irony: the one who challenged Moses' authority over a woman (his Cushite wife) is struck with the mark that most fully embodies exclusion and marginalization. The punishment mirrors the accusation.
11: Aaron's confession: ˀal-naˀ tashset ˁalenu ḥattaˀt (אַל-נָא תָשֵׁת עָלֵינוּ חַטָּאת) — 'please do not lay on us the sin we have foolishly committed.' The word noˀalnu (נוֹאַלְנוּ, we have acted foolishly) is from yaˀal = to act foolishly/presumptuously. Aaron's intercession through Moses for Miriam is the first use of Moses' mediation by the very ones who challenged it.
12: Aaron's vivid plea: let her not be like a met (מֵת, dead one), whose flesh is half consumed when coming out of the womb — a stillborn with putrefying flesh. The graphic image (unusually visceral for biblical prose) communicates the horror of Miriam's condition: she is like a corpse that has been in the process of dissolution. The imagery draws on the connection between skin disease and death-uncleanness in Lev 13-15.
13: wayitsˁaq Mosheh ˀel-YHWH leˀmor: ˀel naˀ rfaˁ naˀ lah (וַיִּצְעַק מֹשֶׁה אֶל-יְהוָה לֵאמֹר: אֵל נָא רְפָא נָא לָהּ) — 'Moses cried out to YHWH: O God, please heal her, please!' Five Hebrew words. The shortest healing prayer in Scripture. The repetition of naˀ (please, I beg) twice in five words concentrates urgency. Moses intercedes instantly and wholeheartedly for the woman who attacked him — a paradigm of forgiveness that precedes intercession.
14: YHWH's response: if her father had merely yaro (יָרֹק, spit) in her face, she would be in disgrace seven days — how much more now? The qal waḥomer (light-to-heavy argument, a fortiori): if the lesser insult requires seven days, the divine rebuke requires seven days. The seven days in Miriam's case is not purely punitive but structural: the whole community waits for her. Her authority in Israel is not destroyed but humbled and restored.
15: wehaˁam loˀ nasaˁ ˁad-heyˀaseph Miryam (וְהָעָם לֹא נָסַע עַד-הֵאָסֵף מִרְיָם) — 'the people did not set out until Miriam was gathered back in.' The whole community's journey halted for her. Despite the discipline, Miriam's honor and communal belonging are preserved. The seven-day exclusion has a firm endpoint: restoration, not permanent exile. The leader who was humbled is brought back in before Israel moves on.
16: The itinerary verse: from Hazeroth to the wilderness of Paran. The movement continues after Miriam's restoration. The crisis of authority resolved, Israel advances toward Canaan — the next crisis awaits at the border in ch. 13. The wilderness formation alternates between provision and testing, always moving forward.
